symptom: Checkout attempts are failing, and the payment service may be rejecting charges. Please investigate the payment path.
expected_signal: payment service error logs during 2026-04-02T22:15:53 to 22:20:55: 'Payment request failed. Invalid token. app.loyalty.level=gold' (6 occurrences)

symptom: We may be unable to complete purchases right now. Customers can browse, but orders are not going through during checkout. Find the cause quickly.
expected_signal: Error traces from load-generator on checkout flows only: ops {'user_checkout_single', 'user_checkout_multi'} with sample trace reaching checkout PlaceOrder before failure, plus payment error logs showing 'Payment request failed. Invalid token.'

symptom: Users report that they can add items to cart but get blocked when trying to place an order.
expected_signal: load-generator shows 6 error traces for checkout operations ('user_checkout_single', 'user_checkout_multi'), while payment logs contain charge failures: 'Payment request failed. Invalid token.'

symptom: Automated synthetic checks for the checkout journey are failing in the latest window.
expected_signal: Tempo contains 6 error traces from load-generator for checkout scenarios with avg_duration 32ms, and the backing service with explicit errors is payment: 'Payment request failed. Invalid token.'

symptom: Something is wrong specifically in the payment path. Charge processing appears broken.
expected_signal: Direct evidence in payment logs: 'Payment request failed. Invalid token. app.loyalty.level=gold' repeated during the incident window

symptom: Customers say checkout fails immediately rather than hanging. There is no obvious platform-wide slowdown, but orders are not completing.
expected_signal: Checkout-related error traces are short (avg 32ms), indicating fast failure, and payment logs show the concrete cause: invalid token causing payment request failure

symptom: Browsing still seems available, but purchase completion is impacted. Please identify which backend dependency is breaking order placement.
expected_signal: Most visible failures are in checkout synthetic traces, while other available metrics do not show broad platform distress; payment is the service with explicit charge errors in logs

symptom: Place-order requests are failing somewhere in the checkout stack.
expected_signal: Sample error trace spans show frontend calling oteldemo.CheckoutService/PlaceOrder successfully up to checkout preparation, while payment logs during the same window report 'Payment request failed. Invalid token.'

symptom: Potential revenue-impacting incident: users can reach the storefront, but conversions appear to be failing in the final purchase step.
expected_signal: The only explicit application failure logs are in payment, and synthetic error traces are concentrated in checkout user journeys rather than browse flows

symptom: Checkout is failing, but shipping metrics look normal. Investigate the next likely dependency in the order flow.
expected_signal: Prometheus shows shipping request rate and p99 latency present without abnormality in the provided window, while payment logs contain repeated request failures due to invalid token

symptom: Synthetic users are failing on both single-item and multi-item checkout paths.
expected_signal: load-generator error traces exist for ops {'user_checkout_single', 'user_checkout_multi'}, and the corresponding backend with concrete errors is payment: 'Payment request failed. Invalid token.'

symptom: Frontend components are active, but something in the transaction path is preventing successful order completion.
expected_signal: Log volume is highest at frontend-proxy, but the actionable error logs are in payment; sample traces pass through frontend and checkout before encountering the failed payment dependency

symptom: Charge RPC failures are being reported for payment requests. Determine why all charges are failing.
expected_signal: payment log evidence: repeated 'Payment request failed. Invalid token. app.loyalty.level=gold' during the exact failure window

symptom: The checkout service appears implicated because order placement attempts are failing, but the break may be in a downstream dependency.
expected_signal: Trace samples show checkout PlaceOrder in the path for failing requests, yet the downstream service with explicit errors is payment, which logs invalid-token request failures

symptom: A user says: 'Everything works until the very last step, then I can't complete my purchase.'
expected_signal: Incident traces with errors are concentrated in checkout journeys, and payment logs confirm the root cause with repeated invalid-token payment failures

symptom: Orders are failing quickly rather than timing out. Please find the backend component causing the immediate checkout failure.
expected_signal: Checkout-related error traces average only 32ms, indicating immediate failure, and the definitive backend error is in payment logs: 'Payment request failed. Invalid token.'

symptom: A customer reports that one product detail page fails to load, but browsing the rest of the storefront seems normal.
expected_signal: Tempo shows error traces for frontend GET requests to /api/products/{productId}, and the trace path ends in product-catalog via oteldemo.ProductCatalogService/GetProduct for the failing product request.

symptom: We are seeing errors in product page requests from the frontend-web service. Can you determine which backend dependency is causing them?
expected_signal: Tempo contains 27 error traces on frontend-web GET requests during the window, and sample spans show the failing request calling frontend -> ProductCatalogService/GetProduct -> product-catalog.

symptom: Alert: frontend product-detail GET requests are erroring during the 22:22-22:28 window.
expected_signal: The strongest signal is the set of frontend-web GET error traces whose downstream span is product-catalog: oteldemo.ProductCatalogService/GetProduct.

symptom: Product browsing is flaky for some items, but there is no broad site-wide outage. Please identify the failing microservice.
expected_signal: Only requests for product details show trace failures, and those traces consistently terminate at product-catalog GetProduct rather than other services; product-catalog log volume is also elevated to 44 vs 20 in baseline.

symptom: The synthetic user_browse_product journey is failing intermittently even though most services look quiet. Investigate the source.
expected_signal: Tempo shows an error trace rooted at load-generator:user_browse_product, with downstream spans flowing through frontend and into product-catalog GetProduct for the failing browse action.

symptom: An engineer says the issue seems related to ProductCatalogService/GetProduct calls. Confirm the real cause.
expected_signal: Trace spans explicitly show frontend invoking oteldemo.ProductCatalogService/GetProduct and the request reaching product-catalog on failing product-page requests.

symptom: We are getting complaints that clicking certain product links returns an error page, while homepage and general browsing still work.
expected_signal: Tempo error traces are concentrated on GET requests for /api/products/{productId}; the failing dependency in those traces is product-catalog GetProduct, indicating item-specific catalog lookup failure.

symptom: Dashboard observation: frontend-proxy log volume is much higher than normal during the incident window, and users report item-page issues. Find the backend root cause.
expected_signal: Although frontend-proxy logs spike to 118, the proving signal is distributed traces showing frontend product-detail requests failing when calling product-catalog GetProduct; product-catalog logs are also elevated to 44 from a 20 baseline.

symptom: Manager escalation: this does not look like checkout or shipping, but product viewing is impacted for at least part of the catalog. What service is actually broken?
expected_signal: Shipping metrics remain ordinary, while trace failures are tied to product detail GET flows and terminate at product-catalog GetProduct, isolating the issue to product-catalog rather than downstream purchase services.

symptom: Alert: user-facing GET requests are erroring, but latency is not dramatically elevated. Identify the failing component.
expected_signal: Frontend-web has 27 error traces with modest average duration (~97ms), and sample traces show the error path is a fast failure in product-catalog GetProduct rather than a slow dependency.

symptom: A product-details API route in the frontend is failing: /api/products/[productId]/index. Determine whether the frontend itself is at fault.
expected_signal: The trace for the failing frontend API route shows the frontend code executes normally and then calls ProductCatalogService/GetProduct, after which the request reaches product-catalog and fails there.

symptom: Customers say 'some products won’t open' rather than 'the site is down.' Investigate this partial browse failure.
expected_signal: The item-specific nature is confirmed by error traces on /api/products/{productId}; traces point to product-catalog GetProduct, matching a failure on a specific product ID instead of a broad frontend outage.

symptom: We suspect a catalog backend regression because product-catalog activity roughly doubled versus normal. Please verify.
expected_signal: Loki shows product-catalog log volume increasing to 44 from a healthy baseline of 20, and Tempo confirms failing product-page requests end at product-catalog GetProduct.

symptom: Monitoring note: there are frontend GET errors, but no strong evidence that shipping, fraud, or other asynchronous systems changed. Where should we focus?
expected_signal: Prometheus shows shipping and fraud metrics close to baseline, while Tempo error traces for frontend GET requests consistently implicate product-catalog GetProduct as the failing dependency.

symptom: Executive update request: isolate whether this is a storefront rendering issue or a backend product data issue affecting individual items.
expected_signal: The storefront path in traces is healthy until the frontend calls ProductCatalogService/GetProduct; the terminal backend span is product-catalog, proving a backend product data lookup failure rather than a pure rendering bug.

symptom: A responder notes that load generation can still hit the site, but a browse-product flow sometimes fails almost immediately. Find the root cause service.
expected_signal: Tempo includes a load-generator error trace for user_browse_product, and the downstream spans show the failure occurs on the frontend product request when invoking product-catalog GetProduct.

symptom: Alert: Kafka consumer lag is elevated for the fraud-detection pipeline during the current incident window. Please investigate whether message processing is backed up.
expected_signal: Prometheus shows kafka_consumer_lag for fraud-detection at about 493.9697 during 2026-04-02T22:30:01 to 22:35:03, indicating a backed-up Kafka consumer.

symptom: SRE report: accounting is suddenly emitting many "Order parsing failed" errors and order processing looks unhealthy. Find the underlying platform issue.
expected_signal: Loki shows 78 accounting error logs with message "Order parsing failed" in the failure window, and the queue-side indicator is elevated Kafka consumer lag on fraud-detection (~493.9697), pointing to Kafka backlog rather than an isolated accounting bug.

symptom: Manager escalation: checkout-related processing seems stuck in the backend even though the site is still responding. We need to know what shared dependency is slowing downstream consumers.
expected_signal: The strongest shared-failure signal is Prometheus kafka_consumer_lag on fraud-detection (~493.9697) together with a burst of downstream accounting errors (78 "Order parsing failed" logs), which is consistent with Kafka queue overload causing lag.

symptom: End-user complaint: orders appear to go through on the website, but follow-up backend processing seems delayed or inconsistent. Please investigate the order pipeline.
expected_signal: Frontend components still have activity, but accounting logs are dominated by 78 "Order parsing failed" errors while Kafka consumer lag remains high (~493.9697 for fraud-detection), indicating a queue-processing issue in Kafka.

symptom: Alert: abnormal spike in accounting errors during order consumption. Determine whether the problem is local to accounting or upstream in messaging.
expected_signal: Accounting has 78 error logs saying "Order parsing failed" during the window, and the corroborating infrastructure signal is elevated kafka_consumer_lag (~493.9697), implicating Kafka backlog as the upstream cause.

symptom: SRE dashboard note: frontend-proxy log volume has jumped noticeably, but there are no useful error traces. Investigate whether requests are piling up behind asynchronous processing.
expected_signal: Loki shows frontend-proxy logs increased to 125 from a healthy baseline of 54, while traces do not show a clear service exception path; the concrete backend signal is elevated Kafka consumer lag (~493.9697), pointing to queue overload.

symptom: Manager escalation: multiple teams report odd order-handling behavior, but nobody sees a clear HTTP failure. Check for an internal platform bottleneck affecting consumers.
expected_signal: There are no decisive error traces, but accounting emits 78 "Order parsing failed" logs and Prometheus shows sustained Kafka consumer lag (~493.9697 on fraud-detection), which identifies Kafka as the shared bottleneck.

symptom: End-user report: the storefront loads, but order-related actions feel unreliable behind the scenes. There are no obvious page crashes. Find the backend cause.
expected_signal: Frontend services still show activity, yet accounting records 78 "Order parsing failed" errors and Kafka consumer lag is elevated (~493.9697), indicating the issue is in Kafka-backed asynchronous processing rather than page serving.

symptom: Alert: investigate message-consumer backlog affecting downstream order workflows. The symptom may present as accounting failures rather than direct Kafka errors.
expected_signal: Prometheus directly reports kafka_consumer_lag around 493.9697 for fraud-detection during the incident, and Loki shows accounting failing repeatedly with 78 "Order parsing failed" entries, consistent with a Kafka queue overload.

symptom: SRE handoff: we see a burst of backend log noise, especially in accounting, but no trace-level smoking gun. Please identify the real root cause service.
expected_signal: The root-cause indicator is the Kafka metric: kafka_consumer_lag is elevated (~493.9697). The downstream symptom is 78 accounting "Order parsing failed" logs, which fits consumer lag and queue overload in Kafka.

symptom: Manager question: why are downstream business services misbehaving when the frontend still looks mostly alive? Look for shared infrastructure stress, not just service-specific code bugs.
expected_signal: Healthy-looking frontend activity contrasts with backend processing issues: frontend-proxy log volume rises to 125, accounting throws 78 parsing errors, and the only direct platform stress signal is Kafka consumer lag at ~493.9697.

symptom: End-user complaint: pages are accessible, but some order follow-up actions seem delayed and inconsistent after submission. Investigate asynchronous components.
expected_signal: The incident window shows elevated Kafka consumer lag (~493.9697 on fraud-detection) alongside repeated accounting failures (78 "Order parsing failed" logs), which indicates delayed/overloaded queue consumption in Kafka.

symptom: Alert: accounting order-consumed path is erroring, but there are almost no useful error traces. Determine whether the problem originates in event transport.
expected_signal: Traces provide little detail, but Loki shows one accounting error trace on 'order-consumed' plus 78 accounting "Order parsing failed" logs, and Prometheus shows elevated Kafka consumer lag (~493.9697), implicating Kafka event transport.

symptom: SRE investigation request: product browsing still appears active, yet order-processing signals are degraded. Find the queue or stream component responsible.
expected_signal: Log volume remains high in frontend-proxy (125) and product-catalog (37), showing the site is active, while accounting accumulates 78 parsing errors and Kafka consumer lag is elevated (~493.9697), identifying Kafka as the degraded stream component.

symptom: Manager escalation: this does not look like a total outage, but some backend workflows are clearly falling behind. Identify the central dependency causing lag across consumers.
expected_signal: The proving signal is Prometheus kafka_consumer_lag at ~493.9697 during the incident window; the downstream manifestation is repeated accounting "Order parsing failed" errors (78 logs), which is consistent with a Kafka queue backlog.

symptom: End-user message: the shopping site works, but after placing an order the system feels slow to reflect follow-up processing. Please investigate the event pipeline.
expected_signal: Frontend-facing services still emit normal activity, but backend order consumption is failing in accounting (78 "Order parsing failed" logs) while Kafka consumer lag stays elevated (~493.9697), pointing to Kafka queue overload as the root cause.

symptom: Alert: CPU usage for the ad service spiked sharply during 2026-04-02 22:37-22:42. Please confirm whether ad is the source of the current frontend instability.
expected_signal: The decisive signal is a CPU saturation spike in the ad service during the failure window, matching the enabled adHighCpu fault.

symptom: SRE report: the ad service looks unhealthy after a fault injection change. Frontend traffic is showing some GET errors, but there are no useful error traces. Investigate the likely cause.
expected_signal: Ad service CPU is abnormally high in the same window; frontend-proxy and frontend-web only show a small number of error traces, making ad CPU the primary causal signal.

symptom: Manager escalation: we deliberately enabled an experiment and now suspect the advertising path is degrading the site. Can you verify if the ad service is causing this incident?
expected_signal: The proving signal is elevated CPU on ad immediately after the adHighCpu flag was toggled, with no stronger competing error signal in logs, metrics, or traces.

symptom: Alert from service dashboard: ad is the only service suspected by the active incident flag, while frontend-proxy and frontend-web are seeing a handful of GET-related errors. Determine the root cause.
expected_signal: CPU usage on ad spikes during 22:37:05-22:42:07; the observed frontend errors are secondary and there are no error traces pointing to another backend service.

symptom: End users report that parts of the storefront feel flaky and some page loads are not completing cleanly, but checkout is not obviously broken. Please investigate.
expected_signal: Frontend-proxy and frontend-web show some GET error traces during the window, and the underlying cause is a CPU spike in ad consistent with the adHighCpu fault.

symptom: SRE handoff: we are seeing elevated frontend-proxy log volume compared with normal, plus a few frontend GET/click errors, but no clear backend error trace. Find the most likely source.
expected_signal: Frontend-proxy log volume rose to 119 from a 54-log baseline and frontend-web/proxy show limited error traces, while the primary root-cause signal is high CPU on ad during the same interval.

symptom: Monitoring dashboard observation: several frontend components look noisier than usual, especially the proxy, yet standard metrics do not show a strong issue in shipping or fraud-detection. Investigate what is actually failing.
expected_signal: Shipping request rate and p99 latency do not indicate the main problem, and fraud-detection lag is near baseline; the differentiating signal is CPU exhaustion in ad during the incident window.

symptom: Manager update request: customer-facing pages seem intermittently unstable, but the usual suspects like checkout, shipping, and fraud pipelines do not look dramatically worse. What service should we focus on?
expected_signal: Observed metrics do not show a meaningful anomaly in shipping or fraud-detection, while ad experiences the key resource anomaly: high CPU coincident with the incident.

symptom: End-user complaint: the website sometimes loads oddly and interactions feel inconsistent, but there is no single hard error message to follow. Please root-cause this.
expected_signal: There are a few frontend GET/click error traces and elevated frontend-proxy log volume, but the conclusive diagnosis comes from a CPU spike in ad during the same period.

symptom: SRE page: there is mild user-visible instability on the storefront, no error traces from backend services, and a couple of noisy but likely unrelated logs from otelcol-contrib and product-reviews. Investigate.
expected_signal: The otelcol-contrib scrape errors and product-reviews llmRateLimitError=False logs are not strong causal signals; the key evidence is sustained high CPU in ad while other services lack a better failure signature.

symptom: Alert triage note: frontend GET requests are showing some failures, but traces do not identify a broken downstream span and service metrics are mostly quiet. Determine the hidden root cause.
expected_signal: Despite the lack of backend error traces, ad shows the primary fault signal: CPU saturation during the exact failure window after the adHighCpu toggle.

symptom: Manager escalation: users say the storefront is degraded, but the dashboards only show scattered frontend symptoms and a lot more proxy logs than usual. We need the most likely service owner to page.
expected_signal: Frontend-proxy log volume increased substantially over baseline, but the service to page is ad because its CPU is the root-cause anomaly aligned with the incident interval.

symptom: SRE note: product-catalog and frontend-proxy are both logging more than normal, yet there are no convincing error traces and shipping looks roughly normal. Identify the origin of the disturbance.
expected_signal: Product-catalog logs rose to 46 from a 20-log baseline and frontend-proxy to 119 from 54, but these are secondary effects; the strongest causal signal is high CPU on ad.

symptom: End-user report: browsing the store is inconsistent right now—pages sometimes feel incomplete or unstable even though there is no explicit checkout failure. Please investigate the likely backend issue.
expected_signal: The user-facing evidence is limited to small numbers of frontend GET/click errors; the root-cause proof is the ad service CPU spike during 22:37:05-22:42:07.

symptom: Alert: frontend-web recorded a few error traces on GET and click operations, but there is no obvious downstream service emitting matching trace errors. Find the service actually causing the disruption.
expected_signal: Frontend-web shows 6 error traces and frontend-proxy 9, but no backend error traces; ad CPU saturation is the underlying signal that explains these edge symptoms.

symptom: Manager question: is this a real customer-impacting backend issue or just observability noise? We saw collector scrape errors, some frontend errors, and increased log volume in proxy and catalog.
expected_signal: The collector scrape errors are incidental, and product-reviews logs are benign feature-flag messages; the real backend issue is CPU exhaustion in ad during the incident window, which best explains the frontend-side symptoms.
